research.title: React Compiler Is Here: Stop Writing useMemo and useCallback
research.whyTrending: React 19's compiler cuts unnecessary re-renders by 25-40%; 'use compiler before manual memo' is now official Vercel guidance and invalidates years of tutorial advice.

seo.suggestedTitle: Stop Writing useMemo: The React Compiler Cleanup Playbook

gaps: Most say remove useMemo but dont give a SAFE removal PROCEDURE
The effect-dependency reference-stability trap (removing a useMemo breaks a useEffect dep) is the real danger and rarely covered
The compiler bail-out on mismatched manual memo is underexplained
No guidance on removing in reviewable waves vs big bang

officialSources.note: official compiler docs and eslint plugin

expertInsights: React Compiler 1.0 stable Oct 2025; default in major frameworks
Existing useMemo/useCallback dont break — they become redundant; compiler drops unnecessary ones
Compiler bails out of optimizing a component if manual memo does not match its inferred memoization
Remove redundant memo only after linting and confirming no effect deps rely on that reference stability
Edge cases still needing manual memo: try/catch blocks, class components (unprocessed), third-party hooks returning non-memoized objects (e.g. React Query useMutation object)
Compiler caches return values of hooks; hidden side effects in third-party hooks during render can break
commonProblems: Deleting a useMemo that a useEffect depends on -> effect now runs every render
Assuming 100% coverage; try/catch and class components excluded

strategy.angle: A removal playbook, not a hype piece: the compiler is stable, so the real question is HOW to safely delete years of manual memoization. Center the one genuinely dangerous move — removing a useMemo/useCallback that a useEffect dependency array relies on for reference stability — and give a wave-based, reviewable procedure with the lint gate
strategy.title: Stop Writing useMemo: A Safe React Compiler Cleanup Playbook
strategy.excerpt: The React Compiler is stable and it memoizes better than you do by hand. But mass-deleting useMemo is where teams break production — usually via a useEffect that quietly depended on reference stability. Here is the safe, reviewable procedure to remove manual memoization for good.
hooks: Contrarian: delete useMemo now is right advice and dangerous advice at the same time
Gotcha: the useMemo you delete might be the only thing keeping a useEffect from firing every render
Practical: remove in reviewable waves gated by the compiler eslint rule, not a big-bang find-replace
outline: Hook: the innocent useMemo deletion that broke a useEffect
Why the compiler makes manual memo redundant
The one dangerous case: reference stability effect deps
The safe removal procedure (waves + lint gate)
What NOT to remove: the real edge cases
